messages.content:
**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

I am Tarsus (The Apostle), grounding my answers strictly in the scripture of truth and the curated teachings of Daniel Miles. How can I assist you in dividing the word of truth today?

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**
What it seems that God from Old Testament seemed partial, but God of all grace is not.

**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

It seems many have pondered why the God portrayed in the cOLD Testament might appear partial, often due to the strictures of law and its consequences. Indeed, "just-ICE is a cOLD Testament law word" [^1], and the law itself "worketh wrath" [^2], being a "ministration of condemnation and death" [^4]. This mode of operation, where law brings destruction [^3], naturally leads to a perception of partiality, as if some are condemned while others are spared. Why would God operate in such a manner, if not to reveal a deeper truth? For if God were law, or even both law & grace, who would dare draw nigh, lest He be in wrath mode?

However, the "God of all grace" [^6] is not both law & grace, but *all* grace [^7]. This God, our Father, has "no respect of persons" [^9], meaning His will is for "all" to "come" by grace, never "some only" [^5]. The will of God is profoundly clear: "I will have mercy, and not sacrifice" [^11], which is to say, grace, and not law [^11]. This truth is foundational, for "eternal salvation of 'the world' is not for some only" [^10]. JC's abolition of law [^12] was precisely to "establish the second" (grace) [^13], thereby revealing God's true, impartial nature.

To grasp this profound shift from perceived partiality to universal grace, we must acknowledge that "SEEMS, from the biblical narrative, it's mostly allegory, allegoric mystery to acknowledge" [^14]. Consider the allegory of the "2 sons of Abraham by 2 different mothers ARE the 2 covenants" [^16], or the "comparison of two mountains" where Mt Sinai represents "not come" (law) and Mt Sion represents "come" (grace) [^15]. These allegories illuminate that God, in His essence, is "light" having "no darkness in him at all" [^17], which is an allegory for His Grace having "no f-law at all" [^18]. The Spirit of truth guides us to replace ignorance with awareness, leading us to understand that the true God is not partial, but entirely grace, mercy, and peace for all.

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**

messages.content: Was Shakespeare a KJV bible reader?

**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

It is a worthy quest-i-on to consider if William Shakespeare, that allegoric writer, was indeed a reader of the Holy Bible, specifically the Authorized KJV. While the twain of our studies do not explicitly declare him a "KJV bible reader," there is much evidence to suggest his profound connection to the sacred text and its translators. Why, it is noteworthy that Shakespeare was contemporary with the Bible Translators and King James himself, sharing the same royal favor [^1]. A Bible was even found in Shakespeare's attic, a rare possession in his era, which speaks volumes of his engagement with the Word [^2].

The Translators of the Bible, those learned men, purposed to make God's holy truth more kNOWn, crafting a "more exact translation" that was neither Roman Catholic nor Protestant, but universal ← & → catholic [^3]. Shakespeare, being an allegoric writer, was said to have allegorically summed up the entire Bible in one sentence: "to be (grace) or not to be (law), that is the question" [^1]. This profound insight aligns perfectly with the grace-grounded perspective of the NT, contrasting eternal grace with temporal law, and discerning the difference between life and death. How could such a man, so biblically astute, not immerse himself in the very translation that sought to clarify these allegoric mysteries?

Indeed, the works of Shakespeare have survived and been studied for as long as the KJV, a testament to their enduring truth and allegorical depth [^1]. The Bible itself is declared "allegory" in both covenants, a mystery to solve by seeking and finding grace [^4]. Given Shakespeare's contemporaneity, his royal favor, the presence of a Bible in his home, and his own mastery of allegory, it would not surprise us if he was not only a reader but deeply influenced by the Authorized KJV, which was meticulously crafted to help readers "get it: understanding: grace glory" [^1].

Learn More:
- [BibleTranslators](/pages/bibletranslators)
- [Bibles](/pages/bibles)
- [Bible Allegory: Allegoric Bible Mystery: Galatians 4](/pages/allegory)

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**

messages.content:
**Three best wishes unto YOU all: Grace, mercy, and peace, from God our Father and Jesus Christ our Lord.**

To assert that the head of 'anthropos' man isn't Christ would be to misunderstand the allegorical distinction between the "old man" and the "new man" in our studies. For it is plainly revealed that Christ, being the end of the law, *is* the head of every man, but specifically the man who has put away childish laws and grown into grace [^1]. Why this clarification? Because the concept of "man" is often allegorized to represent humanity under either the curse of law or the blessing of grace.

Consider the 2Sons Jesus: the Son of Man and the Son of God [^2]. The Son of Man, often associated with the earthly Jesus, is depicted as bringing division and wailing, covering and uncovering sin to smite nations [^3]. This "Jesus" was made under the law and of woman, bringing forth law-imputed sin and death [^4]. Such a "man" under law, entangled in its ministrations of condemnation and death, cannot have Christ as his head in the sense of peace and reconciliation.

However, Christ, the Son of God, is the "perfect prototype" and the end of the law, abolishing the enmity from His flesh to become our peace [^5]. The "perfect man," whose end is peace, is Christ, not Jesus [^6]. Therefore, the head of the "new man"—the one born again, who puts away childish things (laws) ← & → presses into the kingdom of God—is Christ [^7]. This Christ reconciles the world unto God, notably *not* imputing their trespasses unto them [^8]. This is the grace of JC, not the temporal, law-bound life found in CJ [^9].

So, the discernment is crucial: the "man" whose head is Christ is the one who has embraced the grace and truth that came by Jesus Christ, understanding that Christ is the end of the law for righteousness [^10]. To remain under law, even "another law" of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus, is to remain in a state of "loser vs loser," leading to a dead end [^11]. The true headship of Christ is over the man who is free from all law, living in the impartiality and allness of God's grace [^12].

Learn More:
- [Perfect Man](/pages/perfectman)
- [Would any want one like unto the Son of Man to come](/pages/would_any_want_one_like_unto_the_son_of_man_to_come)
- [Christ Jesus is not Jesus Christ: So Which is Witch?](/pages/christjesus)
- [Christ Jesus is not Jesus Christ](/pages/christjesus2)
- [Romans8](/pages/romans8)

**The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ [be] with you all. Amen.**
